classes-and-instances.label: Classes and Instances
classes-and-instances.summary: Learn how a class acts like a blueprint and how each instance is its own object with its own data.

sketch0.title: A class is a blueprint, and an instance is one real object

sketch1.title: Each instance can hold its own data

sketch2.title: How to talk about classes and instances clearly

try_classes_and_instances_sketch0.title: Try it yourself
try_classes_and_instances_sketch0.prompt: A later feature will need to create `Robot` objects. Start with the smallest working version: define a class named `Robot` and leave it empty for now.

try_classes_and_instances_sketch1.title: Try it yourself
try_classes_and_instances_sketch1.prompt: Now make each `Book` remember its own details. Define `Book` with an `__init__` method that takes `title` and `pages`, then store them on the instance.

try_classes_and_instances_sketch2.title: Try it yourself
try_classes_and_instances_sketch2.prompt: Give a `Lamp` enough information to describe itself. Store the incoming `color` on the object, then add `describe()` so it returns `Lamp color: ...` with that stored value.

ci-create-empty-class.title: Create a simple class
ci-create-empty-class.prompt: A later feature will need to create `Robot` objects. Start with the smallest working version: define a class named `Robot` and leave it empty for now.
ci-create-empty-class.hint: You only need the class header and a valid empty body.

help.concept: A class definition creates the blueprint. At this stage, the important part is that Python can recognize the class and construct objects from it later.
help.hint_2: If the class has nothing inside yet, use one placeholder statement so the class body is valid.

ci-create-empty-class.starterCode:
class Robot:
    # Define the class body

sc-class-vs-instance.title: Class or instance?
sc-class-vs-instance.prompt:
In this code, what is `cat1`?

```python
class Cat:
    pass

cat1 = Cat()
```
sc-class-vs-instance.hint: Look at the line that calls `Cat()` to create something.

help.concept: Calling a class like `Cat()` creates one instance of that class.
help.hint_1: `Cat` is the blueprint name, and `cat1` stores the result of creating one object from it.
help.hint_2: Ask yourself which name refers to the template and which name refers to the created object.

mc-true-about-instances.title: True statements about instances
mc-true-about-instances.prompt: Choose all statements that are true about Python instances.
mc-true-about-instances.hint: Think about whether two objects from one class can still hold different data.

help.concept: Instances are separate objects created from the same class, and each instance can store its own attribute values.
help.hint_1: A class can create many objects, and those objects do not have to share the same attribute values.
help.hint_2: Look for statements that describe separate objects made from one blueprint, not statements that confuse the class with one object.

options.a: A class can be used to create multiple instances.
options.b: Different instances of the same class can store different attribute values.
options.c: An instance is the blueprint used to create a class.
options.d: Every instance of a class is automatically the exact same object.

dr-create-instance-steps.title: Order the steps to create an instance
dr-create-instance-steps.prompt: Put these steps in the correct order for defining a class and then creating one instance from it.
dr-create-instance-steps.hint: You need the class before you can create an object from it.

help.concept: Python must know the class definition before you call the class name with parentheses to create an instance.
help.hint_1: First define the class, then finish its body, then create the object.
help.hint_2: The line with `Thing()` belongs after the class block is complete.

tokens.t2: Add an indented class body such as `pass`

fb-call-class.title: Complete the instance creation line
fb-call-class.prompt: Fill in the missing code to create an instance from the `Dog` class.
fb-call-class.hint: Use the class name followed by parentheses.

help.concept: To create an instance, write the class name and call it like a function with parentheses.
help.hint_1: The missing part is the class name, not the variable name.
help.hint_2: The line should read like assigning a new object created from `the missing term`.

ci-add-init-attributes.title: Add attributes with __init__
ci-add-init-attributes.prompt: Now make each `Book` remember its own details. Define `Book` with an `__init__` method that takes `title` and `pages`, then store them on the instance as `self.title` and `self.pages`.
ci-add-init-attributes.hint: Inside `__init__`, copy each incoming value onto `self` with the same name.

help.concept: Constructors set up one new object at a time. Storing values on `self` is what lets each instance keep its own data.
help.hint_1: The method header should accept `self`, `title`, and `pages`.
help.hint_2: Give the object one title attribute and one pages attribute.

sc-init-purpose.title: What does __init__ do?
sc-init-purpose.prompt: What is the main job of `__init__` in a beginner class like `Book` or `Student`?
sc-init-purpose.hint: Think about what happens right when you create an instance.

help.concept: `__init__` is the initializer method that sets up a new instance, often by storing starting values in attributes.
help.hint_1: It runs during object creation, not later when you print or compare objects.
help.hint_2: Look for the choice about setting up the new object's starting data.

options.a: It automatically prints every attribute in the output panel.
options.b: It sets up a new instance when the class is called.
options.c: It turns every variable into a class.
options.d: It deletes old instances before a new one is created.

mc-identify-class-parts.title: Identify the class parts
mc-identify-class-parts.prompt:
Choose all parts of this code that are instance attributes.

```python
class Player:
    def __init__(self, name, score):
        self.name = name
        self.score = score
```

help.concept: Instance attributes are the names attached to `self`, because they belong to each created object.
help.hint_1: Parameters like `name` and `score` are inputs, but the stored attributes are the names after `self.`.
help.hint_2: Find the two names that would be accessed later as `player.something`.

dr-init-lines-order.title: Order the constructor lines
dr-init-lines-order.prompt: Arrange these code pieces into the correct order for a simple constructor.
dr-init-lines-order.hint: Start with the class line, then the method line, then the attribute assignment.

help.concept: A constructor sits inside the class body, and attribute assignments inside the constructor use `self` to store values on the instance.
help.hint_1: The class header comes first, and the indented method definition comes next.
help.hint_2: The assignment using `self.name` belongs inside `__init__`, after the method header.

fb-self-attribute.title: Complete the attribute assignment
fb-self-attribute.prompt: Fill in the missing part so the constructor stores the parameter on the instance.
fb-self-attribute.hint: Read the sentence around the blank and decide what role the missing word plays.

help.concept: The blank should be filled with the term that makes the statement accurate.
help.hint_1: Look at the words before and after the blank to infer what kind of term is needed.
help.hint_2: Pick the choice that completes the statement with the clearest meaning.

ci-instance-method.title: Create a class with a method
ci-instance-method.prompt: Give a `Lamp` enough information to describe itself. Store the incoming `color` on the object, then add `describe()` so it returns `Lamp color: ...` using that stored value.
ci-instance-method.hint: One part of the class stores the color, and the method reads that value back from the instance.

help.concept: A method becomes useful when it works with data already stored on the object. Here, `describe()` should build its return value from `self.color`.
help.hint_1: Save the constructor argument to `self.color` first.
help.hint_2: Return a string that starts with `Lamp color: ` and then adds the stored color.

ci-class-roster-practice.title: Practice instance data with a class label
ci-class-roster-practice.prompt: Add one more class-and-instance practice task. Define `Plant` with `name` and `height`. Store both values on the instance, then add `label()` so it returns text like `Fern - 12 cm`.
ci-class-roster-practice.hint: The constructor should save the data, and the method should read it back from `self`.

help.concept: A class becomes useful when each instance can keep its own data and later use that data in a method result.
help.hint_2: Build the label string from the stored attributes, not from standalone variables.

classes-instances-idea.title: A class is a blueprint, and an instance is one real object
classes-instances-idea.bodyMarkdown:
A **class** is a way to describe what a kind of object should be like. An **instance** is one actual object made from that class.

Think of a class like a cookie cutter and an instance like one cookie made from it. The cutter describes the shape. Each cookie is a separate thing.

Here is a simple Python example:

```python
class Dog:
    pass

my_dog = Dog()
friend_dog = Dog()
```

Step by step:

- `class Dog:` creates a new class named `Dog`.
- `pass` means the class is empty for now.
- `my_dog = Dog()` creates one instance of `Dog`.
- `friend_dog = Dog()` creates another instance.

Even though both objects come from the same class, they are two different instances.

Worked example:

```python
class Book:
    pass

book1 = Book()
book2 = Book()

print(type(book1))
print(book1 == book2)
```

What this shows:

- `book1` and `book2` are both instances of `Book`.
- They are not the same object, so `book1 == book2` is not automatically `True`.

Create a class named `GameCharacter`, then create two instances with different variable names. Click Run and inspect the output if you print their types.

instances-store-data.title: Each instance can hold its own data
instances-store-data.bodyMarkdown:
A useful class usually gives each instance its own **attributes**. Attributes are values stored on an object.

A common way to set them up is with `__init__`, which runs when you create an instance.

```python
class Student:
    def __init__(self, name, grade):
        self.name = name
        self.grade = grade
```

Line by line:

- `def __init__(self, name, grade):` defines the setup method.
- `self` means "this specific instance."
- `self.name = name` stores the given name on the instance.
- `self.grade = grade` stores the given grade on the instance.

Worked example:

```python
class Student:
    def __init__(self, name, grade):
        self.name = name
        self.grade = grade

ava = Student("Ava", 92)
ben = Student("Ben", 85)

print(ava.name)
print(ben.grade)
```

This creates two `Student` objects. Each one keeps its own values.

- `ava.name` is `"Ava"`
- `ben.grade` is `85`

Change one value, such as `"Ben"` to `"Mia"` or `85` to `90`, and predict what the output panel will show before you click Run.

classes-vs-instances-language.title: How to talk about classes and instances clearly
classes-vs-instances-language.bodyMarkdown:
Beginners often mix up the class itself with one instance of the class.

Example:

```python
class Car:
    def __init__(self, color):
        self.color = color

red_car = Car("red")
blue_car = Car("blue")
```

Correct language:

- `Car` is the **class**.
- `red_car` is an **instance** of `Car`.
- `blue_car` is another **instance** of `Car`.
- `color` is an **attribute** on each instance.

Notice that both instances use the same class, but they can store different data.

Worked example:

If you check `red_car.color`, you get `"red"`.
If you check `blue_car.color`, you get `"blue"`.

That is the main power of classes: one blueprint, many separate objects.

Imagine a `BankAccount` class. Name two different instances you could create, and decide what data each one should store.

Line by line: read each statement from top to bottom. Notice which names are created, which values are passed into functions, and what output the code is meant to show.
